abstract: Abstract Professions continue to be the primary means through which societies institutionalize expertise. Recent analyses and narratives predict that artificial intelligence (AI) will make meaningful inroads into non‐routine reasoning about complex cases, threatening the authority of professions. These predictions, we argue, draw on substantialist understandings of expertise as an intellectual possession, a mental achievement, or a cognitive state performed – by humans or machines – to achieve effects. A synthesis of empirical studies shows that expertise is more accurately conceptualized as relationally constituted – generated, applied, and recognized – through interactions. Relational expertise creates challenges of opacity, translation, and accountability for the development and deployment of AI technologies in the context of professional work. A relational understanding of expertise disrupts notions that professions may be augmented with, subordinated to, or dismantled by AI technologies. Instead, AI technologies are embedded in the network of interactions through which the relational expertise of professions is constituted.

ob.ai_reason: AI与专业知识关系，AI核心议题
ob.tldr: 研究表明，专业知识并非独立认知状态，而是通过人际互动生成、运用与认可的关系性构成。此种关系性专业知识导致AI在专业场景面临不透明、翻译与问责难题。AI并非简单取代或辅助，而是嵌入专业互动网络，需重新审视其在组织中的角色与治理。

title: Relational Expertise: What Machines Can't Know
title_zh: 关系性专业知识：机器无法知晓之事
